title: Sleep Duration, Quality & Health Impact
source: CDC, NIH NHLBI, National Sleep Foundation, Lancet

chunks.title: Recommended Sleep Duration by Age (CDC/NIH)
chunks.content:
CDC and National Sleep Foundation recommended sleep per 24 hours:
- Newborns (0-3 months): 14-17 hours
- Infants (4-12 months): 12-16 hours (including naps)
- Toddlers (1-2 years): 11-14 hours (including naps)
- Preschool (3-5 years): 10-13 hours (including naps)
- School-age (6-12 years): 9-12 hours
- Teens (13-18 years): 8-10 hours
- Adults (18-60 years): 7 or more hours (CDC) / 7-9 hours (NSF)
- Older adults (61-64): 7-9 hours
- Seniors (65+): 7-8 hours
Consistently sleeping <6 hours or >9 hours associated with increased mortality, cardiovascular disease, and metabolic disorders. Source: CDC, NIH NHLBI, Hirshkowitz et al. (2015).

chunks.title: Sleep Stages and Architecture
chunks.content:
Normal sleep cycles through stages every 90-110 minutes, repeating 4-5 times per night:
- N1 (light sleep): 5% of total sleep. Transition from wakefulness. Easy to wake.
- N2 (deeper light sleep): 45% of total sleep. Heart rate slows, body temperature drops.
- N3 (deep/slow-wave sleep): 25% of total sleep (~1.5-2h for 8h sleep). Physical restoration, tissue repair, immune function, growth hormone release. Most important for feeling rested.
- REM (rapid eye movement): 25% of total sleep. Dreaming, memory consolidation, emotional processing.
Deep sleep (N3) decreases with age — adults over 60 may get only 10-15% deep sleep. Source: NIH NHLBI, Healthline, American Academy of Sleep Medicine.

chunks.title: Sleep Quality Assessment Indicators
chunks.content:
Signs of GOOD sleep quality:
- Fall asleep within 15-20 minutes of lying down
- Sleep through the night (0-1 brief awakenings)
- Wake feeling refreshed and alert
- Consistent sleep/wake schedule (±30 min daily)

Signs of POOR sleep quality:
- Takes >30 minutes to fall asleep (sleep onset latency)
- Wake up 3+ times per night
- Lie awake >20 minutes after waking during the night
- Daytime fatigue despite adequate hours
- Rely on caffeine to stay alert

The PSQI (Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index) scores ≤5 = good sleep, >5 = poor sleep, based on 7 components. Sleep regularity (consistent bed/wake times) may predict mortality better than duration (Lancet 2023). Source: Buysse et al. (1989), Lancet Public Health (2023).

chunks.title: Sleep Hygiene Best Practices (CDC/NIH)
chunks.content:
Evidence-based sleep hygiene recommendations:
1. Consistent schedule: Same bedtime and wake time daily, including weekends (±15 min).
2. Dark, cool room: 18-20°C (65-68°F) ideal. Use blackout curtains.
3. No screens 30-60 min before bed: Blue light (460nm) suppresses melatonin production by up to 50%.
4. No caffeine after 2 PM: Caffeine half-life is 5-6 hours. Even small amounts can disrupt sleep architecture.
5. No large meals 2-3 hours before bed: Digestion raises core body temperature, opposing sleep onset.
6. No alcohol within 3 hours of bed: Alcohol reduces REM sleep and causes fragmented sleep in the second half of the night.
7. Exercise regularly but not within 2-3 hours of bedtime.
8. Bedroom = sleep only: Don't work, eat, or watch TV in bed (stimulus control therapy).
9. Relaxation routine: Reading, warm bath, meditation, deep breathing.
10. Limit naps to 20-30 minutes before 3 PM. Source: CDC, NIH NHLBI, Sleep Foundation.
